structure.title: Candidate for the degree of Doctor of Philosophy
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from an ordinance (No. 11) of the Rajiv Gandhi Proudyogiki Vishwavidyalaya Bhopal, India, outlining the regulations for the Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree. The ordinance is based on the UGC (University Grants Commission) Regulation 2009 and has been amended up to August 2011. 

The main points covered in this section include:
- Eligibility criteria for Ph.D. candidates, stating that they must hold a Master's degree with a minimum of 60% marks (55% for SC/ST candidates) from a recognized university.
- Application requirements, including the need to specify qualifications, proposed subject/discipline, and thesis title, as well as providing details of the chosen supervisor and research center from the university's approved lists.
- Eligibility to work for a Ph.D. degree in a subject related to the candidate's Master's or Bachelor's degree in Engineering, Technology, or Applied Sciences, provided that the subject is taught at the university and the research center is recognized.
- The possibility of pursuing research in allied or interdisciplinary subjects, with the decision on subject alignment resting with the Academic Council.
- Clarification that departments of the university's constituent college (UIT-RGPV) are considered University Teaching Departments.

structure.title: Application for registration for Ph.D. degree
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from an ordinance (No. 11) issued by Rajiv Gandhi Proudyogiki Vishwavidyalaya, Bhopal, India, regarding the Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree program. The ordinance outlines the regulations and eligibility criteria for candidates applying for Ph.D. admission, as per the UGC (University Grants Commission) Regulation 2009.

Key points:
- Candidates must hold a Master's degree with a minimum of 60% marks or an equivalent grade from a recognized university. SC/ST candidates have a relaxed requirement of 55% marks.
- Applicants need to submit a prescribed form for Ph.D. registration, providing details such as qualifications, proposed subject/discipline, thesis title, and supervisor/co-supervisor preferences.
- The document specifies that candidates will typically work for their Ph.D. in the subject they have studied at the Master's or Bachelor's level in Engineering, Technology, or Applied Sciences.
- The subject must be included in the university's list of subjects, and teaching should be offered in that subject within the university's teaching departments or affiliated colleges.
- Research in allied subjects of an interdisciplinary nature is permitted, and the Academic Council will decide on the eligibility of such subjects.
- The ordinance provides specific instructions for candidates regarding the choice of supervisors, research centers, and the submission of relevant certificates and fellowship details.

structure.title: Qualifications and experience
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the requirements and regulations for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree at Rajiv Gandhi Proudyogiki Vishwavidyalaya Bhopal, as per the UGC Regulation 2009. It provides a structured framework for candidates seeking admission to the Ph.D. program, covering eligibility criteria, application procedures, and research guidelines.

The document highlights that candidates must hold a Master's degree with a minimum of 60% marks or an equivalent grade, with a relaxed criterion of 55% for SC/ST candidates. The application process involves submitting a prescribed form with details of qualifications, proposed research subject, and the desired supervisor from the university's approved list.

Eligibility is primarily based on the candidate's Master's or Bachelor's degree subject, which should align with the university's list of subjects and active teaching departments. Research in allied subjects of interdisciplinary nature is permitted, with the Academic Council making the final decision on subject eligibility.

The document also mentions the need for candidates to provide certificates of qualifying examinations, fellowship grants, and contact details. It sets the foundation for a comprehensive Ph.D. admission process, ensuring a fair and structured evaluation of prospective researchers.

structure.title: Proposed subject/discipline and relevant faculty
structure.summary:
The partial document is an excerpt from an ordinance (No. 11) of Rajiv Gandhi Proudyogiki Vishwavidyalaya Bhopal, outlining the regulations for the Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program as per UGC (University Grants Commission) guidelines from 2009. The main points covered in this section are:

- Eligibility Criteria: Candidates must hold a Master's degree with a minimum of 60% marks or an equivalent grade, with a relaxation of 55% for SC/ST candidates.
- Application Process: Applicants must submit a prescribed form with details including qualifications, proposed subject/discipline, thesis title, and the name of the supervisor and research center from the approved university lists.
- Eligibility to Work for Ph.D.: Students are typically permitted to pursue a Ph.D. in the subject they have studied at the Master's or Bachelor's level in Engineering, Technology, or Applied Sciences, provided the subject is included in the university's statute and is taught in a university teaching department or affiliated college.
- Interdisciplinary Research: The ordinance allows for research in allied subjects of an interdisciplinary nature within the same or allied faculties, with the Academic Council making the final decision on subject alignment.
- Recognition of Constituent College Departments: Departments of the university's constituent college (UIT-RGPV) are considered as university teaching departments for the purpose of this ordinance.

The ordinance is based on the UGC (University Grants Commission) Regulation 2009 and has been amended up to August 2011. 

Key points:
- Eligibility: Candidates must hold a Master's degree with at least 60% marks (55% for SC/ST candidates) from a recognized university.

structure.title: Availability of seats
structure.summary: This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for admissions to a Ph.D. program, specifically focusing on Ordinance No. 11 as per UGC Regulation 2009. It details the availability of seats for the Ph.D. program, emphasizing the limits on the number of scholars a supervisor can oversee. The document then describes the admission process, which is managed by an Admission Committee consisting of academic faculty members. The committee's responsibilities include setting entrance exams, conducting interviews, and selecting candidates based on qualifications and performance. The document also mentions preferences for candidates with specific fellowships and the process for conducting entrance tests, including the syllabus and duration. The admissions are held twice a year, with a specific timeline provided for the admission process.

structure.title: Admission Procedure

structure.title: Interview and Allotment
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the interview and allotment process for PhD candidates, as per Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009. It specifies the composition of the interview board, which includes the Vice Chancellor, Dean of Faculty, Chairman of Board of Studies, Head of University Teaching Departments, recognized supervisors, and subject experts. The quorum and chairmanship provisions are also mentioned.

The text details the interview process, including the venue, order of candidate interviews, and the expected discussion topics. It assigns weightage to different categories of candidates, with varying emphasis on interview performance, written entrance exam scores, and postgraduate exam aggregate marks.

The document further explains the allotment process, where the board finalizes the list of admitted candidates, prioritizing those with UGC/CSIR/DST fellowships or teacher fellowships, followed by candidates based on merit in the entrance exam. In cases of equal marks, the percentage in the qualifying postgraduate exam will determine merit.

structure.title: Fees
structure.summary: The text is a concise instruction to generate a summary of a partial document, specifically requesting a description of the main points covered. It emphasizes the direct and focused nature of the task, aiming to provide a clear and structured overview of the document's key elements.

structure.title: Course work
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for the Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, as per Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009. It details the admission process, fee structure, and coursework requirements.

In the admission process, the allotment board aims to accommodate students' preferences for research locations, supervisors, and thesis titles. Students can change their thesis titles with prior approval from the Research Degree Committee (RDC). The admission process includes an entrance test and interviews, and a merit list is prepared accordingly.

The fee structure for the Ph.D. program includes registration fees, tuition fees, library fees, laboratory fees, caution money, identity card fees, and development fees. Fees are payable to both the university and the research centre, and the fee amounts may vary as announced by the university.

Regarding coursework, Ph.D. students are required to complete a two-semester course as part of their pre-Ph.D. preparation. The first semester focuses on research methodology, quantitative methods, computer applications, and an elective. In the second semester, students choose two electives and submit a research review or survey to their supervisors for evaluation.

structure.title: Research Centre
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on the examination process and research centres. It mentions that university examinations will be held at the end of each semester, and the scheme will be communicated by the university. The candidate's review or survey will be evaluated by a supervisor, who will determine its satisfaction. To successfully complete the course, candidates must achieve a minimum of 65% in semester examinations or an equivalent grade, and earn at least 10 credits during the coursework.

The document also discusses research centres where candidates can conduct their Ph.D. research. It mentions that research can be carried out in university teaching departments, affiliated autonomous government/government-aided engineering colleges, or research centres in affiliated colleges with accredited undergraduate and postgraduate courses. The permission for research centres is granted for a maximum of two years, and institutions must reapply for fresh permission with a progress report and fee before the registration period expires. Existing study centres are also subject to these conditions and must comply within three months of the ordinance's effectiveness.

Additionally, candidates may be allowed to conduct research at recognized research institutes, industries of international repute with sufficient R&D infrastructure, or government-approved R&D centres, provided they are recognized by the university's executive council. If a candidate works in an industry recognized by the university, they are required to have at least one supervisor or co-supervisor from that industry, holding a position equivalent to an associate professor at the university.

structure.title: Supervisor/Co -supervisor
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and requirements for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on the role of supervisors and the research process. 

It mentions that candidates can work in research establishments like CSIR or DRDO labs, and they must have at least one supervisor or co-supervisor from the industry, who should be a scientist/director with a minimum rank of Associate Professor. 

The document then lists the qualifications for supervisors/co-supervisors, including their academic position, doctorate degree, research publications, patents, and experience. It also states that supervisors must maintain research productivity to remain eligible for enrolling new candidates. 

The text further describes the process of submitting a research synopsis and making an oral presentation to the Research Degree Committee (RDC). It provides details about the committee's composition, including the Kulpati, Dean, Chairman, Head of Departments, and external experts. The RDC meeting schedule and quorum requirements are also mentioned. 

Overall, this section of the document provides guidelines for Ph.D. candidates and supervisors, emphasizing research standards, academic qualifications, and the evaluation process.

structure.title: RDC
structure.summary:
This section of the document outlines the regulations and procedures for obtaining a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on the role of supervisors and co-supervisors. 

It states that teachers in affiliated colleges who were previously recognized as supervisors/co-supervisors by another university can continue in that role if they meet the requirements outlined in Section 11(a). However, individuals currently enrolled in a Ph.D. program at the university are ineligible to act as supervisors or co-supervisors.

To become a recognized supervisor/co-supervisor, an individual must apply using the prescribed form, endorsed by their institution's head. The Research Degree Committee (RDC) will make the decision regarding recognition.

After completing the Pre-Ph.D. coursework, candidates can submit a synopsis of their proposed research, along with a thesis title, signed by their supervisor and institution head. The synopsis must be submitted in ten copies, each page signed by the candidate, supervisor, co-supervisor, and institution head.

Candidates are then required to present their proposed research orally to the RDC, which consists of several members, including the Kulpati (or nominee), Dean of the faculty, Chairman BoS, Head/Professor of the University Teaching Department, and an external subject expert. The external expert and two other members form a quorum.

The document also notes that supervisors may be allowed to observe their candidate's oral presentation upon request, and no travel or daily allowances will be provided for candidates and supervisors attending the RDC meeting.

The RDC meets twice a year to consider applications for supervisor/co-supervisor recognition, as well as other related matters.

structure.title: Submission of synopsis and title of thesis
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for obtaining a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on the recognition of supervisors and the process of submitting a research proposal. 

It states that teachers in affiliated colleges who were previously recognized as supervisors or co-supervisors can continue in their roles if they meet the specified requirements. However, individuals currently enrolled in a Ph.D. program at the university are ineligible to act as supervisors or committee members. 

To become a recognized supervisor, an individual must apply using the prescribed form, endorsed by their institution's head. The Research Degree Committee (RDC) will make the decision regarding recognition. 

After completing the necessary coursework, candidates are eligible to submit a synopsis of their proposed research, along with a thesis title, signed by their supervisor and institutional head. The synopsis must be submitted in ten copies, each page signed by the candidate, supervisor, co-supervisor, and head of the institution. 

The candidate is then required to present their proposed research orally to the RDC, which consists of several key members, including the Kulpati (or nominee), Dean of the faculty, Chairman BoS, Head or Professor of the University Teaching Department, and an external subject expert. The external expert and two other members form the quorum. 

The document also mentions that supervisors may be allowed to observe their candidate's oral presentation upon request, and no travel or daily allowances will be provided for attending the RDC meeting. 

Finally, it notes that the RDC meetings will be held twice a year at the university office to consider applications for supervisor recognition.

structure.title: Oral presentation of proposed work
structure.summary:

It states that teachers in affiliated colleges who were previously recognized as supervisors or co-supervisors can continue their roles if they meet the specified requirements. However, individuals currently enrolled in a Ph.D. program at the university are ineligible to act as supervisors or committee members. 

To become a recognized supervisor, individuals must apply using the prescribed form, endorsed by their institution's head. The Research Degree Committee (RDC) will make the decision regarding recognition. 

After completing the necessary coursework, candidates can submit a synopsis of their proposed research, along with a thesis title, signed by their supervisor and institution head. The synopsis must be submitted in ten copies, each page signed by the candidate, supervisor, co-supervisor, and institution head. 

Candidates are then required to present their proposed research orally to the RDC, which consists of several university members, including the Kulpati or their nominee, the Dean, and subject experts. The external expert and two other members form the quorum for the committee. 

The document also mentions that supervisors may request to be present during their candidate's oral presentation as an observer, and no travel or daily allowances will be provided for attending the RDC meeting. 

The RDC meets twice a year to consider applications for supervisor recognition and review research proposals.

It begins by stating that teachers in affiliated colleges who were previously recognized as supervisors or co-supervisors can continue in their roles if they meet the specified requirements. However, individuals currently enrolled in a Ph.D. program at the university are ineligible to act as supervisors. 

The document then explains the process for individuals seeking recognition as supervisors/co-supervisors, requiring them to submit an application in a prescribed format, which will be reviewed by the Research Degree Committee (RDC). 

The RDC plays a crucial role in evaluating candidates' proposed research work, including the synopsis, title, and oral presentation. The committee's composition is detailed, and it is responsible for recommending eligible supervisors and maintaining a list of approved supervisors with their specializations. 

After RDC approval, candidates can register for the Ph.D. degree, and the document outlines the process for thesis submission, including timelines and the consequences of non-compliance. It emphasizes the importance of regular communication between the candidate, supervisor, and the university's administrative bodies. 

The document also mentions the period for thesis submission, which is a minimum of 24 months after course completion and a maximum of five years from registration, with the possibility of a one-year extension under certain conditions. 

Overall, this section of the document provides a comprehensive guide to the Ph.D. supervision and research process, ensuring a structured and regulated academic journey for candidates.

structure.title: Change in thesis title
structure.summary: The text is a concise instruction for a task, likely a writing or content generation assignment. It requests a summary of a partial document, specifically focusing on the main points and key topics covered. The instruction emphasizes a direct and succinct response, excluding any unnecessary details or additional text.

structure.title: Change in synopsis
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for obtaining a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on the role of supervisors and the research process. 

It begins by stating that teachers in affiliated colleges who were previously recognized as supervisors or co-supervisors can continue in their roles if they meet the specified requirements. However, individuals currently enrolled in a Ph.D. program are ineligible to act as supervisors. Those seeking recognition as supervisors must apply through a prescribed process, with their application reviewed by the Research Degree Committee (RDC).

The document then details the steps a candidate must follow after completing their pre-Ph.D. coursework. They are required to submit a synopsis of their proposed research, along with a thesis title, which is then presented orally to the RDC. The committee consists of several members, including the Kulpati, Dean, and subject experts. The RDC evaluates the application and makes decisions regarding the approval of the thesis title, synopsis, and supervisors.

Once the RDC approves the research proposal, the candidate is registered for the Ph.D. degree, and a registration letter is issued. The candidate is expected to pay regular fees and complete their research within a specified timeframe. The RDC may suggest changes to the synopsis, and the candidate is given a chance to revise and resubmit. If major revisions are required, the candidate may need to improve and reappear before the RDC.

The document also mentions that the RDC ensures the eligibility of supervisors and co-supervisors, and they should be affiliated with approved research centers. Finally, it outlines the period for thesis submission, allowing for an extension under certain conditions.

structure.title: Major revision required
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, as per Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009. It details the responsibilities of various committees and authorities in the Ph.D. registration and supervision process. 

Key points include:
- The Research Degree Committee (RDC) is responsible for evaluating and approving the thesis title, synopsis, and supervisors. They also ensure the eligibility of supervisors and co-supervisors, maintaining a list of approved personnel.
- After RDC approval, the candidate is registered for the Ph.D. degree, with the registration date determined by the fee payment. The Registrar issues the registration letter and informs all relevant parties.
- If the RDC suggests changes, the candidate must revise and resubmit the synopsis, and the Dean of the Faculty approves the revisions.
- In cases of major revisions or unsatisfactory oral presentations, candidates may be asked to improve and reappear before the RDC.
- If a candidate is not recommended for Ph.D. registration, they are informed, and their caution money is refunded.
- The RDC ensures that supervisors are from approved research centers and meet specific criteria.
- Candidates have a specified period of 24 months to several years to complete and submit their thesis, with the possibility of extension under certain conditions.

structure.title: Non-recommendation for registration
structure.summary: The text is a brief instruction to generate a summary of a partial document, emphasizing the main points. It is a concise directive for a specific task, likely intended for an AI assistant or a similar tool.

structure.title: Supervisor/Co-supervisor requirements
structure.summary:
This section of the document outlines the regulations and procedures for obtaining a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on the recognition of supervisors and the process of submitting a research proposal. 

It states that teachers in affiliated colleges who were previously recognized as supervisors/co-supervisors can continue their roles if they meet the specified requirements. However, individuals registered for a Ph.D. degree at the university are ineligible to act as supervisors or committee members. 

To become a recognized supervisor, individuals must apply using the prescribed form, endorsed by their institution's head. The Research Degree Committee (RDC) will make the decision regarding recognition. 

After completing the Pre-Ph.D. coursework, candidates can submit a synopsis of their proposed research, along with a thesis title, signed by their supervisor and institution head. The synopsis must be submitted in ten copies, with signatures on every page. 

Candidates are then required to present their proposed research orally to the RDC, which consists of the Kulpati (or nominee), Dean of the faculty, Chairman BoS, Head/Professor of the University Teaching Department, and an external subject expert. The external expert and two other members form the quorum. 

The document also mentions that supervisors may be allowed to observe their candidate's oral presentation upon request, and no travel or daily allowances will be provided for attending the RDC meeting. 

Finally, it notes that the RDC meetings will be held twice a year to consider applications for supervisor recognition.

structure.title: Period for submission of thesis
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, as per Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009. It details the responsibilities of various committees and authorities in the Ph.D. registration and supervision process. 

Key points include:
- The Research Degree Committee (RDC) will recommend eligible supervisors/co-supervisors, create a list of approved supervisors with their specializations, and ensure they meet the requirements.
- After RDC approval of the thesis title, synopsis, and supervisors, the candidate's registration date is set, and they are enrolled as a student, required to pay fees regularly.
- Changes in the thesis title or synopsis are communicated to the candidate, and revised submissions are reviewed by the Dean of the Faculty.
- If major revisions are needed, the candidate may be asked to improve and reappear before the RDC.
- If a candidate is not recommended for Ph.D. registration, their caution money is refunded.
- The RDC ensures supervisors are from an approved research center.
- Candidates must submit their thesis within five years of registration, with a possible one-year extension granted by the Kulpati if requested in time.

structure.title: Submission timeline
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, as per Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009. It covers various aspects of the Ph.D. program, including the appointment of supervisors, registration process, thesis submission, and research requirements. 

The document details the role of the Research Degree Committee (RDC) in approving the thesis title, synopsis, and supervisors. It mentions the eligibility criteria for supervisors and the process of registration for Ph.D. candidates, including fee payment and the issuance of registration letters. The text also outlines the timeline for thesis submission, allowing a minimum of 24 months and a maximum of five years, with provisions for extension and re-registration. 

Additionally, it covers the requirements for teacher candidates, attendance criteria, and the process for changing supervisors under special circumstances. The document emphasizes the importance of regular progress reports, attendance records, and fee payments, with consequences outlined for unsatisfactory progress or non-compliance. The submission of a thesis summary, research papers, and the appointment of examiners are also mentioned as part of the Ph.D. program's requirements.

structure.title: Submission timeline for teacher candidates
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on registration, thesis submission, and supervision. 

It mentions Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009, providing guidelines for Ph.D. candidates. The text highlights the importance of timely thesis submission within the registration period, with options for extension and re-registration upon payment of prescribed fees. 

For teacher candidates with five years of teaching experience, a reduced thesis submission period of 18 months is allowed. The document emphasizes the required attendance of 240 days, with relevant declarations and certificates to be included in the thesis. 

Supervision-related aspects are covered, including the process for changing supervisors under special circumstances and the submission of six-monthly reports by supervisors to track the scholar's attendance, fee payment, and research progress. 

The document also mentions the submission of a thesis summary, research papers, and the appointment of examiners as part of the Ph.D. evaluation process.

structure.title: Attendance requirements
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on registration, thesis submission, and supervision. It mentions Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009, providing guidelines for Ph.D. candidates.

The text highlights the importance of timely thesis submission within the registration period, with provisions for extension and re-registration. It specifies that candidates must submit their thesis within five years, and if they fail to do so, their registration will be cancelled. Re-registration is allowed with a fee, and candidates have four years to submit their thesis after re-registration.

For teacher candidates with five years of teaching experience, there is a reduced minimum period of 18 months for thesis submission. The document also emphasizes the required attendance of 240 days at the research centre with the supervisor, and the need for relevant declarations and certificates to be included in the thesis.

Supervision-related matters are addressed, including the process for changing supervisors under special circumstances and the requirement for six-monthly reports on attendance, fees, and research progress. The university's scrutiny of these reports is mentioned, and consequences for unsatisfactory progress or non-compliance are outlined.

Lastly, the document touches on the submission of a thesis summary, research papers, and the appointment of examiners, indicating the final stages of the Ph.D. process.

structure.title: Change of supervisor
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on the thesis submission process and related requirements. 

It mentions Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009, which provide guidelines for Ph.D. candidates. The text highlights the importance of timely thesis submission within the registration period, with options for extension and re-registration upon payment of prescribed fees. 

Candidates are required to meet specific conditions, such as a minimum attendance of 240 days and a certain period of research work, before submitting their thesis. Special provisions are made for teacher candidates with five years of teaching experience, allowing them to submit their thesis earlier. 

The document also covers the process of changing supervisors under special circumstances, the submission of six-monthly reports by supervisors, and the consequences of unsatisfactory progress or non-compliance with fees. 

Additionally, it mentions the requirement to submit a summary of the thesis, along with a list of research papers published or accepted for publication, as part of the examination process.

structure.title: Six monthly report
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on the thesis submission process and related requirements. It mentions Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009, providing guidelines for Ph.D. candidates.

The text highlights the importance of timely thesis submission within the registration period, with the option to extend the registration and submit the thesis within a specified timeframe. It mentions the re-registration process, allowing candidates to continue their research after five years with certain conditions.

The document also covers special provisions for teacher candidates with five years of teaching experience, reducing the minimum submission period. It emphasizes the attendance requirements, with candidates needing to provide relevant declarations and certificates to prove their research engagement.

Additionally, the document discusses the process of changing supervisors under special circumstances and the submission of six-monthly reports to track the progress of research scholars. It mentions the consequences of unsatisfactory progress, including the removal of the scholar's name from the Ph.D. registration list.

The section concludes with instructions for submitting a summary of the thesis, research papers, and the appointment of examiners, providing a comprehensive overview of the Ph.D. degree requirements.

structure.title: Summary of thesis and appointment of examiners
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, as per Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009. It covers various aspects of the Ph.D. program, including registration, thesis submission, supervision, and examination.

- **Registration and Thesis Submission:** Candidates must submit their thesis within the registration period, with the option to extend and re-register under specific conditions. Re-registration is allowed after five years with a fee, and the candidate has four years to submit the thesis. Teachers with five years of experience can submit their thesis earlier, and all candidates must meet attendance requirements.
- **Supervision:** The document discusses the role of supervisors and the process for changing supervisors under special circumstances. It emphasizes the need for regular progress reports and attendance records.
- **Examination and Evaluation:** Candidates must submit a summary of their thesis, along with research publications, to the Registrar. The appointment of examiners is outlined, ensuring they are from outside the University and with relevant expertise. The process for pre-submission defense is described, including the committee's composition and the requirement for oral presentations.
- **Publication Requirements:** Candidates are expected to publish research papers in peer-reviewed journals, with specific authorship criteria. These publications are to be included in the thesis submission.
- **Administrative Procedures:** The document provides appendices and proformas for various processes, ensuring a structured approach to Ph.D. administration. It also mentions the roles of the Kulpati, Dean, and other academic committees in overseeing the Ph.D. program.

structure.title: Pre Submission Defense Committee (PSDC)
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for the Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, specifically focusing on the selection of examiners and the pre-submission process. 

It details the responsibilities of the candidate, supervisor, and the university's administration in ensuring a fair and rigorous evaluation. The candidate must inform the registrar of their anticipated thesis submission date and provide a draft thesis in both soft and hard copies. The supervisor is tasked with submitting a panel of potential examiners, ensuring they are qualified and from diverse institutions, with a focus on external examiners. If the candidate and supervisor are related, the panel is obtained from a designated head or senior professor.

The Kulpati, or their nominee, plays a key role in appointing two examiners from the proposed panels, seeking their consent by providing a summary and publications. The document also highlights the requirement for candidates to publish research papers in reputable journals, with authorship guidelines, and these publications are to be included in the thesis.

The pre-submission defense process is outlined, where the candidate presents their draft thesis to a committee, including the Kulpati, Dean, and subject experts. This committee evaluates the proposed research, and the candidate must make an oral presentation, forming a crucial part of the Ph.D. assessment process.

structure.title: Submission of draft thesis
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for the Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, specifically focusing on the selection of examiners and the pre-submission process. 

It states that a candidate's supervisor must submit a panel of at least six potential examiners, who are actively involved in the relevant research area and hold a specific academic rank. These examiners should be from outside the university's jurisdiction, with at least half from outside the state and the option to include international examiners. A similar panel is to be submitted by the Dean of the Faculty where the candidate is enrolled. In cases where the candidate and supervisor are related, the panel is obtained from the Head of the University Teaching Department or a senior professor appointed by the Kulpati.

The Kulpati then selects two examiners from these panels, and their consent is obtained by sharing the thesis summary and publications. The document emphasizes the importance of obtaining consent and provides instructions for sending the summary.

Before submitting the final thesis, the candidate must prepare a draft and publish at least two research papers in reputable, peer-reviewed journals, either as the sole author or as the first author in case of co-authored work. The candidate should include reprints or acceptance letters of these publications within the thesis.

The document also mentions the Pre-Submission Defense process, where the candidate presents their draft thesis to the University Teaching Department or a designated location. This defense is conducted before a committee, including the Kulpati or their nominee, the Dean, the Chairman BoS, and the Head or a Professor from the relevant University Teaching Department. The committee's presence is required to form a quorum.

structure.title: Publication of research papers
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for the Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, specifically focusing on the selection of examiners and the pre-submission process. 

It states that a candidate's supervisor must submit a panel of at least six potential examiners, ensuring they are actively involved in the relevant research area and hold appropriate academic ranks. These examiners should be from outside the university's jurisdiction, with at least half from outside the state and the option to include international names. A similar panel is to be provided by the Dean of the Faculty where the candidate is enrolled. In cases where the candidate and supervisor are related, the panel is obtained from the Head of the University Teaching Department or a senior professor appointed by the Kulpati.

The Kulpati then selects two examiners from these panels, and their consent is obtained by sharing the thesis summary and publication list. The document emphasizes the importance of obtaining consent and provides instructions for sending the summary.

Before submitting the final thesis, the candidate must prepare a draft and provide it in both soft and hard copies, adhering to the university's format. Additionally, the candidate is required to publish at least two research papers in reputable, peer-reviewed journals, either as the sole author or as the first author in case of co-authored work. These publications are to be included in the thesis.

The document also mentions the Pre-Submission Defense, where the candidate presents their draft thesis in the University Teaching Department or a designated location. This defense is organized upon the candidate's request, supported by the supervisor. The Pre-Submission Defense Committee consists of the Kulpati or their nominee as Chairman, the Dean of the Faculty, the Chairman BoS of the subject, and the Head or a Professor (or Associate Professor if a Professor is unavailable) from the University Teaching Department. A quorum is formed with the presence of three members.

structure.title: Pre-submission defense
structure.summary:
This partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for the Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, specifically focusing on the selection of examiners and the pre-submission process. 

It details the requirements for candidates to submit a panel of potential examiners for their thesis, with specific criteria regarding their qualifications and geographical distribution. The document also highlights the process for cases where the candidate and supervisor are related, ensuring impartiality. The university's head, or Kulpati, plays a key role in appointing examiners and overseeing the process.

Additionally, the text mentions the need for candidates to publish research papers in reputable journals, providing guidelines for authorship and submission. The pre-submission defense committee (PSDC) is introduced, explaining the oral presentation and defense process, including the committee's composition and quorum requirements. 

Overall, this section of the document provides a comprehensive framework for the Ph.D. examination process, ensuring fairness, academic rigor, and adherence to UGC regulations.

structure.title: Oral presentation before PSDC
structure.summary:
This document outlines the regulations and procedures for obtaining a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on the thesis submission process. It details the requirements for selecting examiners, the role of the supervisor and examination committee, and the steps involved in the pre-submission defense committee (PSDC) evaluation.

Key points include:
- The supervisor and the Dean of the Faculty must submit panels of potential examiners, ensuring they meet specific criteria regarding their academic rank and research expertise.
- If the candidate is related to the supervisor, the panel of examiners is obtained from the Head of the University Teaching Department or a senior professor nominated by Kulpati.
- The Kulpati appoints two examiners from the submitted panels, and their consent is obtained by sharing the thesis summary and publications.
- Candidates must publish research papers in approved journals and include them in the thesis.
- The PSDC evaluation involves an oral presentation by the candidate, followed by feedback and potential revisions to the thesis.
- The candidate has three attempts to present their case to the PSDC, and failure to receive a submission recommendation leads to PhD registration cancellation.
- The finalized thesis must adhere to specific formatting and submission requirements, including hardbound copies and a soft copy on CD.

structure.title: Feedback and comments from PSDC
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for PhD candidates, specifically regarding the oral presentation, thesis submission, and committee evaluations. It defines the term 'relations' for the candidate's family members and provides guidelines for the supervisor's involvement. The document emphasizes the importance of feedback and revisions based on the Pre Submission Defense Committee (PSDC) recommendations. It details the process of thesis submission, including formatting, binding, and the required number of copies. The candidate's registration may be cancelled if they fail to meet the PSDC's requirements after a certain number of attempts.

structure.title: Major revision and resubmission
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for PhD candidates, focusing on the oral presentation and thesis submission process. It defines the term 'relations' for the context of the ordinance. It mentions that supervisors can request to observe their candidate's oral presentation and that no travel expenses will be provided for the Pre Submission Defense Committee (PSDC) meeting. The presentation is open to faculty and students for feedback, and any changes suggested by the PSDC must be incorporated into the thesis draft. The candidate has three attempts to present their work to the PSDC, and if major revisions are required, they must improve and reappear. After PSDC approval, the candidate finalizes and submits their thesis in a specific format, including hard and soft copies. The thesis should be forwarded by the head of the institution where the candidate was registered.

structure.title: Presentation attempts and cancellation

structure.title: Finalization of thesis

structure.title: Thesis formatting and submission
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for obtaining a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, specifically focusing on the submission and evaluation of the thesis. It defines the term 'relations' for the purpose of the ordinance and provides guidelines for the Pre Submission Defense Committee (PSDC) meeting, including the role of the supervisor and the presentation format. structure.title: Evaluation of thesis and viva-voce examination
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the requirements and evaluation process for a Ph.D. thesis as per Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009. It details the necessary components of the thesis submission, including a declaration of originality, supervisor's certificate, and payment of examination fees. The text emphasizes the criteria for thesis acceptance, focusing on research quality, originality, and presentation. It explains the evaluation process, where examiners provide recommendations and detailed feedback, and outlines scenarios for thesis acceptance, revision, or rejection based on the examiners' reports. The document also mentions the viva-voce examination, the role of the supervisor in clarifying thesis content, and the procedures for recalling and replacing examiners if necessary.

structure.title: Thesis requirements for Ph.D. award
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the requirements and procedures for the evaluation and acceptance of a doctoral thesis for the award of a Ph.D. degree. It specifies that the thesis should be an original research contribution, demonstrating the candidate's critical analysis and judgment, and should meet language and presentation standards. The document details the submission process, including necessary declarations, certificates, and fees. It explains the evaluation process, involving two examiners who can recommend acceptance, revision, or rejection, and outlines the steps for each scenario, including the potential involvement of a third examiner. The document also mentions the viva-voce examination, the role of the supervisor in providing clarifications, and the authority of the Kulpati in managing the examination process.

structure.title: Examiners' recommendations and comments

The document emphasizes the criteria for thesis acceptance, focusing on research quality, originality, and presentation. It details the evaluation process, where examiners provide recommendations and detailed feedback, and outlines scenarios for thesis acceptance, revision, or rejection based on the examiners' reports. structure.title: Clarification from supervisor
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the requirements and evaluation process for a Ph.D. thesis as per Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009. The text emphasizes the thesis's originality, research quality, and presentation standards. It describes the examiners' role in recommending acceptance, revision, or rejection, providing detailed feedback and questions for the viva voce examination. structure.title: Recall of thesis and appointment of new examiner
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the requirements and evaluation process for a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree, as per Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009. It details the necessary components of a thesis submission, including a declaration of originality, supervisor certificates, and payment of examination fees. The text emphasizes the importance of the thesis demonstrating original research, critical analysis, and clear presentation. The evaluation process involves sending the thesis to two examiners, who will recommend acceptance, revision, or rejection, and provide detailed feedback. The document also mentions the possibility of a third examiner's opinion in specific cases. It highlights the role of the Kulpati in overseeing the process, including the option to recall a thesis from an examiner and appoint a replacement if necessary. The main focus is on the standards and procedures for assessing and awarding a Ph.D. degree.

structure.title: Thesis rejection by both examiners
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the requirements and procedures for the evaluation and acceptance of a doctoral thesis for the Ph.D. degree. It specifies that the thesis should embody original research, either through the discovery of new facts or a unique interpretation of existing knowledge, and demonstrate the candidate's critical thinking and judgment skills. The document also mandates the inclusion of a declaration of authenticity and a certificate from the supervisor(s).

The evaluation process involves sending the thesis to two examiners, who must provide detailed assessments, including comments on each chapter and a list of questions for the viva-voce examination. The examiners can seek clarifications from the supervisor, and the Kulpati has the authority to recall and reassign the thesis if an examiner fails to submit a report within the given timeframe.

The decision on the thesis is based on the examiners' recommendations, with specific guidelines for acceptance, revision, or rejection. If there is a disagreement between examiners, a third examiner is appointed to provide a final opinion. The document also mentions the submission of fees and 'no dues' certificates as part of the thesis submission process.

structure.title: Revision recommendation by both examiners
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the requirements and evaluation process for a Ph.D. thesis as per Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009. It details the necessary accompanying documents, including a declaration of originality, supervisor's certificate, and fee payment. The text emphasizes the thesis's originality, research quality, and presentation standards. It describes the examiners' role in recommending acceptance, revision, or rejection, providing detailed feedback and questions for the viva-voce examination. The document also covers scenarios where examiners may seek clarifications, fail to submit reports, or disagree in their evaluations, outlining the steps to be taken in each case.

structure.title: Approval by one examiner, rejection or revision by another

It details the necessary accompanying documents, including a declaration of originality, supervisor certificates, and fee payments. structure.title: If the examiners recommend that the candidate be asked to revise/improve his thesis
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the procedures for the final stages of a Ph.D. degree evaluation, focusing on the viva voce examination process. It details the composition of the examination board, including the supervisor, co-supervisor, and examiners, with specific considerations for cases where the candidate is related to the supervisor. The process of appointing external examiners and scheduling the examination is described, along with provisions for special circumstances. The document emphasizes the open nature of the viva voce, where the candidate presents their research, and the board and audience can ask questions. It also covers scenarios where the candidate may need to revise their thesis or appear for a second viva voce examination, including the associated fees and procedures. The role of the Kulpati in overseeing these processes is highlighted throughout.

structure.title: Appearing in other examination
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, specifically focusing on thesis submission, examination, and award processes. It details the requirements for resubmitting a revised thesis, including the need for clear indication of revisions and the involvement of previous examiners. The text explains the decision-making process if examiners have differing opinions on the revised thesis, leading to either acceptance or rejection. It also mentions the role of the Dean in evaluating minor revisions and approving the viva voce examination.

Other points include restrictions on research scholars enrolling in other courses or examinations, publication guidelines for the thesis, the authority of the Vice Chancellor in awarding the Ph.D. degree, and the availability of examiners' reports to successful candidates upon request.

structure.title: Publication of thesis
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, specifically focusing on thesis submission, examination, and award processes. It details the requirements for resubmitting a revised thesis, including mentioning the revision and sending it to the original examiners. If a thesis is revised multiple times, the document explains the decision-making process involving a third examiner. It also mentions the role of the Dean in evaluating minor revisions and approving the viva voce exam.

The text prohibits research scholars from enrolling in other degree programs or examinations, except for specific certificate/diploma courses. It also states that thesis publication requires university permission and must include a note about the Ph.D. award. The document outlines the authority of the Vice Chancellor to notify Ph.D. awards and the distribution of thesis copies within the university and department libraries. Additionally, it mentions providing successful candidates with examiners' reports upon request and fee payment, while maintaining examiner anonymity.

structure.title: Award of Ph.D.
structure.summary: The text is a concise instruction for a task, likely a writing or data analysis assignment. It requests a summary of a document's main points, emphasizing the need for a direct and focused response. The instruction is clear and specific, indicating that the description should be the sole content of the response, without any additional text or explanations.

structure.title: Reports of examiners
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, specifically focusing on thesis submission, examination, and award processes. It details the requirements for resubmitting a revised thesis, including mentioning the revision and sending it to the original examiners. In cases where examiners have differing opinions on the revised thesis, the document explains the steps for involving a third examiner and the consequences of their decision. It also mentions the role of the Dean in evaluating minor revisions and setting up the viva voce examination.

The text prohibits research scholars from enrolling in other degree-granting courses or examinations, except for specific certificate/diploma courses. It also states that thesis publication requires university permission and must include a note on the title page regarding the Ph.D. award.

Additionally, the document describes the authority of the Vice Chancellor to notify Ph.D. degree awards and the subsequent reporting to the Executive Council. It outlines the distribution of thesis copies to libraries and the supervisor. Finally, it mentions that successful candidates can obtain examiners' reports for a fee, with the examiners' identities remaining confidential.

structure.title: Withdrawal of degree
structure.summary: The partial document outlines regulations and provisions for a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, specifically focusing on Ordinance No. 11 and its amendments. It mentions the consequences of plagiarism or irregularities, stating that the university has the right to withdraw the degree in such cases. The text also addresses the transition of rules for candidates already registered or in the process of registration before the new ordinance takes effect, ensuring a fair continuation of their studies under the appropriate regulations.

structure.title: Provision for candidates registered before this Ordinance
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the regulations and procedures for a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, specifically focusing on Ordinance No. 11 and its various provisions. It mentions the consequences of plagiarism or irregularities, stating that the university has the right to withdraw the degree in such cases. The text also addresses the transition of rules for candidates who were registered under previous ordinances and those who have applied for registration but are not yet registered, ensuring a clear framework for all Ph.D. candidates.

structure.title: Provision for candidates who have applied for registration
structure.summary:
This partial document is a section from a university's academic regulations, specifically Ordinance No. 11, which outlines the rules and procedures for the Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program. The main points covered in this part of the document are:

1. Degree Withdrawal: The university reserves the right to withdraw a Ph.D. degree if any form of irregularity or plagiarism is detected. This ensures academic integrity and maintains the credibility of the degree.

2. Transitional Provisions: The ordinance provides guidelines for candidates who were registered under the previous ordinance and those who have applied for registration but have not been registered before the new ordinance came into effect. It ensures a smooth transition for students already in the program and clarifies the requirements for new applicants.

3. Synopsis Proforma: The document includes a proforma for the synopsis of a Ph.D. thesis, detailing the essential elements that a student must include. This covers the title, introduction, literature review, proposed contributions, methodology, expected outcomes, references, and a list of the candidate's published papers. The proforma is designed to guide students in structuring their research proposals and ensure a comprehensive overview of their Ph.D. work.

4. Signatures and Dates: The synopsis proforma requires signatures from the supervisor, candidate, and co-supervisor (if applicable), along with corresponding dates. This section ensures accountability and documentation of the research process.

structure.title: Appendix 1: Proforma for Synopsis
structure.summary: This partial document outlines the requirements for a Ph.D. thesis synopsis, as per the UGC Regulation 2009. It provides a structured format for students to present their research proposal, including the title, introduction, literature review, contributions, methodology, expected outcomes, references, and a list of the candidate's published papers. The document also specifies the need for signatures from the supervisor, candidate, and co-supervisor (if applicable), ensuring a formal approval process for the research plan.

structure.title: Appendix 2: Declaration by the Candidate
structure.summary: A candidate is declaring that their Ph.D. thesis is their original work, completed under the supervision of a specified supervisor at a research centre. They affirm that they have fulfilled the attendance requirements, having spent more than 240 days working with their supervisor. The candidate also states that the thesis does not include any unattributed work from other degrees or universities. This declaration is in accordance with Ordinance No. 11 and UGC Regulation 2009 for the Ph.D. program.

structure.title: Appendix 3: Certificate of the Supervisor
structure.summary: The partial document is a certificate issued by a supervisor for a research candidate pursuing a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) degree. It confirms that the candidate has completed their research work under the supervisor's guidance and has fulfilled the necessary requirements as per the UGC Regulation 2009. The supervisor attests to the candidate's attendance, the originality of the work, its completion, and its adherence to the university's standards for a Ph.D. thesis. The certificate includes signatures from both the co-supervisor and the supervisor, along with dates, to validate the candidate's research and academic achievements.

structure.title: Appendix 4: Confidential Progress Report
structure.summary:
This partial document is a section of a research scholar's progress report for a Ph.D. degree, as per UGC Regulation 2009. It outlines the format for a six-monthly progress report, including the following details:

1. Name of the research scholar.
2. Subject area of research.
3. Ph.D. topic registered.
4. Name and details of the primary supervisor.
5. Name of the co-supervisor, if applicable.
6. A description of the guidance provided by the supervisor on the research topic.
7. The period during which the scholar has been working with the guide, including any leave dates.
8. Remarks and evaluation by the supervisor on the scholar's work.
9. Fee payment details with receipt number and date.
10. Signature, date, and contact information of the supervisor.

The document is a structured template for collecting regular updates on a Ph.D. scholar's progress, ensuring proper supervision and assessment of their research work.

structure.title: Appendix 5: Request for Pre-Ph.D. Presentation
structure.summary: The partial document is a formal request letter addressed to the Registrar of Rajiv Gandhi Proudyogiki Vishwavidyalaya in Bhopal, India, regarding a student's Ph.D. thesis process. The student is seeking approval to conduct a pre-Ph.D. presentation based on their draft thesis. The letter includes essential details such as the candidate's name, supervisor and co-supervisor information, subject area, place of work, and the thesis title. The student has completed their draft thesis and is now requesting the necessary arrangements for the presentation, a requirement before proceeding further in their Ph.D. program, as per UGC Regulation 2009.

structure.title: Appendix 6: Forwarding Letter of Head of Institution
structure.summary:
The partial document is a section of an academic regulation and procedure manual for a Doctor of Philosophy (Ph.D.) program, specifically focusing on the submission and evaluation of a Ph.D. thesis. The main points covered include:

- A forwarding letter from the head of the institution where the candidate was registered for their Ph.D. degree, confirming the submission of the thesis in the required number of copies and the payment of necessary fees.
- The inclusion of a page for the examiner's report on the Ph.D. thesis, which provides a structured evaluation form with options to recommend the thesis for the Ph.D. degree, suggest revisions, or reject the thesis.
- Detailed guidelines for the examiner's report, emphasizing the criteria for assessing the thesis, including the originality of research, critical analysis, language proficiency, and presentation of the subject matter.
- Instructions for the examiner to provide a detailed report with specific feedback, ensuring a comprehensive evaluation of the Ph.D. thesis.

structure.title: Appendix 7: Examiners Report on Ph.D. Thesis
structure.summary: The partial document is a section of an academic evaluation report for a Ph.D. thesis, as per UGC Regulation 2009. It outlines the criteria for assessing the thesis, including the research quality, critical analysis, language, and presentation. The examiner is required to provide a detailed report and indicate whether the thesis is recommended for the Ph.D. degree, requires revisions, or should be rejected. The document also includes a provisional certificate regarding the award of the Ph.D. degree, issued by Rajiv Gandhi Technical University, Bhopal, following the University Grants Commission's regulations.

structure.title: Appendix 8: Provisional Certificate Regarding Award of Ph.D. Degree
structure.summary: The partial document is a certificate issued by Rajiv Gandhi Technical University, Bhopal, regarding the award of a Ph.D. degree to a candidate. It mentions the university's compliance with the UGC Regulation 2009 for the Doctor of Philosophy program. The certificate includes details such as the subject of the Ph.D., the candidate's name, and the title of the Ph.D. thesis. The document is a provisional certificate, indicating that it serves as a preliminary confirmation of the degree award, with further administrative steps possibly required to finalize the process.
